attributes.stem:
A 34-year-old woman presents to the gynaecology clinic with a three-month history of irregular, heavy vaginal bleeding. She has polycystic ovarian syndrome diagnosed five years ago. She has no other medical conditions and takes no regular medications. She is planning to conceive in the future but is not actively trying at present.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index is 40 kg/m2. Pelvic examination reveals no abnormalities. A cervical screening test is normal. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an endometrial thickness of 12 mm with multiple peripheral ovarian follicles bilaterally. Endometrial sampling reveals endometrial hyperplasia without atypia.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Combined oral contraceptive pill with endometrial sampling in 6 months
Observation with repeat ultrasound in 3 months
Levonorgestrel intrauterine system with endometrial sampling in 6 months
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with irregular vaginal bleeding and is found to have endometrial hyperplasia without atypia in the context of polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS). Women with PCOS are at increased risk of endometrial hyperplasia due to chronic anovulation leading to unopposed oestrogen exposure.

Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena) with follow-up endometrial sampling in 6 months is the most appropriate management. The Mirena provides high local concentrations of progestin to the endometrium, leading to regression of hyperplasia in most cases. It also provides effective contraception while allowing future fertility once removed.

Hysterectomy would be inappropriate in this young woman who desires future fertility. While it would definitively treat the endometrial hyperplasia, it is overly aggressive for hyperplasia without atypia and would prevent future pregnancy.

Combined oral contraceptive pill can regulate menstrual cycles in PCOS but is not the preferred treatment for established endometrial hyperplasia, where higher doses of progestin are required.

Endometrial ablation would be inappropriate in this young woman who desires future fertility, as it permanently damages the endometrium and significantly reduces fertility.

Observation alone would be inappropriate as endometrial hyperplasia requires active management to prevent progression and reduce the risk of endometrial cancer.
learningPoints: Endometrial hyperplasia without atypia in young women with PCOS should be treated with progestin therapy, with the Mirena intrauterine system being first-line.
Regular endometrial surveillance is required to ensure regression of hyperplasia.
Women with PCOS have a higher risk of endometrial hyperplasia due to chronic anovulation and unopposed oestrogen.
references: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Management of Endometrial Hyperplasia (Green-top Guideline No. 67). London: RCOG; 2016 [cited 2024 Nov 24]. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/guidance/browse-all-guidance/green-top-guidelines/management-of-endometrial-hyperplasia-green-top-guideline-no-67/
